to integrate the confirmation process with the production process. You can also choose
the sequence in which these details appear on the confirmation screen.
Figure 3.45 Single-Entry Screen of Confirmation
In the Pushbutton Text fields, you can define the text of the pushbuttons to suit your
business needs. Checking the Closed box enables a minimized (closed) view of the
information on the confirmation screen, which can be opened when the production
supervisor is ready for data entry.
Tips and Tricks
To check that settings are correct, with no duplication or other layout error, you can
click the Detail Areas button to make a consistency check. Also, you can click the Pre-
view button to simulate the confirmation screen.
© 

123
3.12 Confirmation
Note
You can activate the CONFPP07 enhancement if there’s a need for more than one reason
for variances. As a practical workaround (without activating the enhancement), on the
screen shown in Figure 3.45 under Detail Areas, you can add an option that says “Addi-
tional data: Reason” as many times as needed from the dropdown list.

sections.title: Time of Confirmation
sections.text:
3.12.4 Time of Confirmation
If there is a large number of confirmations that the production supervisor needs to per-
form on a daily basis, then it’s practical to perform confirmations (such as quantity
updates or backflushes) in the background, when there is less of a workload on the sys-
tem. However, if an immediate update of all quantity and activity data posted is
required, then you should make the necessary settings. These are the same settings
used for the Process Control field at the beginning of Section 3.12.2. To set up the time of
confirmation, follow menu path SAP IMG  Production  Shop Floor Control  Operations
 Confirmation  Single Screen Entry  Define Time for Confirmation Processes or use
Transaction OPKC. On the screen shown in Figure 3.46, choose when the system should
update the following:
 Auto GR (automatic GR)
 Backflush
 Actual Costs
Figure 3.46 Process Control for Confirmation
The available update options are listed here:
 Immediately Online
 Immediately in Update Program
 Later in Background Job
We’ll cover how to set up background jobs in Section 3.16.

3 Discrete Manufacturing Configuration
124

sections.title: Reasons for Variances
sections.text:
3.13 Reasons for Variances
During confirmation against the production order, when the yield, scrap, and rework is
recorded, the user can assign a reason for any variance between planned and actual
production. For example, assume that due to machine malfunction or defective com-
ponents, the actual production was less than planned. In that case, the user can assign
the specific reason for this deviation or variance. Similarly, when entering the scrap
quantity, the user can give the reason for the variance. Later, when executing the pro-
duction order information system (Transaction COOIS) or even when using embedded
SAP Fiori analytics, the user can filter out the information based on the reason for vari-
ance. This helps clarify the reasons behind the problems and helps the production
supervisor take corrective or preventive actions.
As a PP consultant during an SAP S/4HANA implementation project, you should main-
tain a complete list of plant-related reasons for variances and configure them accord-
ingly in the system. You should also perform multiple partial confirmations and enter
different reasons for variance for each deviation.
Note
If there’s a business need to enter multiple reasons for variances, you should engage an
ABAP resource and activate the CONFPP07 enhancement.
You can also assign a user status to a reason for variance to add further control in the
confirmation.
Note
To set up a new status profile, use Transaction BS02.
Figure 3.47 Reasons for Variance
© 

125

sections.title: Defining Print Control
sections.text:
3.15 Defining Print Control
To configure a new reason for the variance for a given plant, follow menu path SAP IMG 
Production  Shop Floor Control  Operations  Confirmation  Define Reasons for Vari-
ances or use Transaction OPK5. Figure 3.47 lists the reasons for variance for Plant 1710.
Once you select a specific reason for variance and click the User Status folder, the system
prompts you to enter the status profile and then the user status.

sections.title: Trigger Points
sections.text:
3.14 Trigger Points
Trigger points are automatic functions that the system can perform when it’s able to
meet a user-defined condition. For example, when a user releases an operation, the sys-
tem can automatically release all preceding operations via a trigger point. Minimal
configuration steps are involved in trigger points: you simply define the trigger point
usage and then define a group for the trigger points.
The Trigger Point Usage field provides a unique identification to list trigger points that
will have a common usage. This enables a production supervisor to quickly select the
relevant trigger point based on its usage. To define the (standard) trigger points usage,
follow menu path SAP IMG  Production  Shop Floor Control  Master Data  Trigger
Point  Define (Standard) Trigger Point Usage. On the initial screen, enter an alphanu-
meric key and give a short description of the trigger point usage.
You can group all of the trigger points together and then assign them to an operation.
Doing so saves you the time and effort involved in individually creating and assigning
trigger points.
To define a group for standard trigger points, follow menu path SAP IMG  Production 
Shop Floor Control  Master Data  Trigger Point  Define Group for Standard Trigger
Points. On the initial screen, enter an alphanumeric key and give a short description of
the trigger points group.
3.15 Defining Print Control
During the production process, there’s often a business need to print several shop floor
papers—for example, a production order, a pick list, and a GI slip. The SAP S/4HANA
system offers standard layouts for each shop floor paper. During an SAP S/4HANA sys-
tem project implementation, you should engage an ABAP resource to develop new
scripts or modify the scripts of the shop floor papers to fulfill a client’s requirements.
We recommend that you to discuss and evaluate all of the shop floor papers needed as
printouts with the client. For example, you can eliminate the layout development of a
confirmation and its printout requirement if there’s an SAP S/4HANA system terminal
from which to make live data entries, or you can use an upload program to automati-
cally enter the confirmation. Furthermore, if Adobe Document Services is installed,
then you can also create PDF printouts of the shop floor papers.

3 Discrete Manufacturing Configuration
126
To make the necessary settings to print shop floor papers, follow menu path SAP IMG 
Production  Shop Floor Control  Operations  Define Print Controls or use Transaction
OPK8. The resulting screen, shown in Figure 3.48, lists the shop floor papers that you
can individually work on and for which you can make the desired settings.
Figure 3.48 Printing Shop Floor Papers
Note
Chapter 6 shows how we used list LG01 (Object list), shown on the right-hand side of
Figure 3.48, to get printouts with the SAP S/4HANA system standard layout.

sections.title: Background Jobs
sections.text:
3.16 Background Jobs
To optimize the SAP system’s resources, which will help to improve performance and
minimize undue burdens, there are several processes that you can set up as back-
ground jobs. A background job enables you to define the frequency with which the sys-
tem automatically performs the desired function without manual intervention. For
example, if MRP is run on a weekly basis and planners are generally satisfied with the
results, then you can set up a background job to automatically convert planned orders
into production orders.
To set up a background job, follow menu path SAP IMG  Production  Shop Floor Control 
Operations  Schedule Background Jobs or use Transaction SM36.
We suggest that you choose the Job Wizard icon and use the wizard because it provides
a step-by-step approach for setting up a background job. Table 3.1 lists the program
names of selected background jobs that you can set up, and the system asks for this
information. Before setting up the background job, you should also set up the variant
of the background job so that the system processes only the relevant data.
© 

127

sections.title: Summary
sections.text:
3.17 Summary
Tips and Tricks
On the initial screen of Transaction SE38, you should enter a program name listed in
Table 3.1 and choose Execute. The system then navigates to the screen where you can
set screen parameters and save them by pressing (Ctrl)+(S) or choosing Goto  Vari-
ants  Save as Variant.
You can read additional documentation on the background program by choosing the
Information icon, where available.
3.17 Summary
The logical and sequential configuration steps that we undertook in this chapter will
enable a production planner and production supervisor to perform the same set of
steps and activities in configuration steps in process manufacturing. If they follow all
of the steps covered in this chapter in a sequential and logical order, then all of the
major business processes and scenarios of production involved in production orders
(shop floor control) will be covered. See Chapter 6 for more details that will align with
the business processes.
The next chapter covers process manufacturing configuration.
Functions Program Names
Convert planned orders into production orders PPBICO40
Print production orders PPPRBTCH
Print shop floor papers PPPRBSEL
Mass processing COWORKDISPATCHNEW
Confirmation: Componentized processes (GR, GI, HR, actual) CORUPROC
Confirmation: Fast entry records CORUPROC1
Table 3.1 Background Jobs

©
